patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 8/8] mm: use zone lock guard in __offline_isolated_pages()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series replaces explicit lock/unlock patterns in the mm/page_alloc.c file with automatic scope-based cleanup using zone_lock_irqsave. The first message introduces this change to the __offline_isolated_pages() function, which is used for offline memory sections.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: The patch author posted the original patch with a suggested-by credit to Steven Rostedt and a signed-off-by line for themselves. The patch introduces a zone lock guard in __offline_isolated_pages() to replace explicit lock/unlock patterns.

version_history.subject: [PATCH 7/8] mm: use zone lock guard in free_pcppages_bulk()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the locking mechanism in the mm module by introducing a zone lock guard in free_pcppages_bulk(). The current explicit lock/unlock pattern is replaced with automatic scope-based cleanup, reducing the risk of deadlocks and improving code readability.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: The patch introduces a zone lock guard in free_pcppages_bulk() to replace the explicit lock/unlock pattern with automatic scope-based cleanup, improving code readability and reducing the risk of deadlocks.

The change replaces an explicit lock/unlock pattern with automatic scope-based cleanup, allowing for direct return from loops and removing unnecessary variables.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: The patch introduces a zone lock guard in take_page_off_buddy() to simplify the code and improve performance. It also removes an unnecessary 'ret' variable and allows for direct return from loops.

The change replaces an explicit lock/unlock pattern with automatic scope-based cleanup, ensuring that dump_page() remains outside the locked section and avoiding potential lockdep splats.

The current implementation uses an explicit lock/unlock pattern with a goto statement, which is replaced with automatic scope-based cleanup. This change improves code readability and reduces the risk of bugs.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary: Suggested simplifying the code by removing unnecessary braces around a single-line return statement.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/8] mm: introduce zone lock guards

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/8] mm: introduce zone lock guards

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces zone lock guards to simplify control flow in mm functions by replacing explicit lock/unlock patterns with automatic scope-based cleanup. The patches are ordered by decreasing value, with the first six simplifying control flow and the last two removing 'flags' and redundant unlock calls.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the additional overhead introduced by the zone lock guards, citing a 32-byte increase in page_alloc.o text size. Suggested that the developers should consider the tradeoff between maintainability and performance.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri,  6 Mar 2026 16:05:35 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> Use the newly introduced zone_lock_irqsave lock guard in
> reserve_highatomic_pageblock() to replace the explicit lock/unlock and
> goto out_unlock pattern with automatic scope-based cleanup.
> 
> ...
>
> -	zone_lock_irqsave(zone, flags);
> +	guard(zone_lock_irqsave)(zone);

guard() is cute, but this patch adds a little overhead - defconfig
page_alloc.o text increases by 32 bytes, presumably all in
reserve_highatomic_pageblock().  More instructions, larger cache
footprint.

So we're adding a little overhead to every user's Linux machine for all
time.  In return for which the developers get a little convenience and
maintainability.

Is it worth it?

review_comments.author: Vlastimil Babka
review_comments.summary: Provided measurements showing a small increase in get_page_from_freelist function size, but suggested that the issue might be due to compiler heuristics rather than the zone lock guards themselves.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 3/6/26 19:00, Steven Rostedt wrote:
> 
> [ Adding Peter ]
> 
> On Fri, 6 Mar 2026 09:53:36 -0800
> Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org> wrote:
> 
>> On Fri,  6 Mar 2026 16:05:35 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:
>> 
>> > Use the newly introduced zone_lock_irqsave lock guard in
>> > reserve_highatomic_pageblock() to replace the explicit lock/unlock and
>> > goto out_unlock pattern with automatic scope-based cleanup.
>> > 
>> > ...
>> >
>> > -	zone_lock_irqsave(zone, flags);
>> > +	guard(zone_lock_irqsave)(zone);  
>> 
>> guard() is cute, but this patch adds a little overhead - defconfig
>> page_alloc.o text increases by 32 bytes, presumably all in
>> reserve_highatomic_pageblock().  More instructions, larger cache
>> footprint.

I get this:

Function                                     old     new   delta
get_page_from_freelist                      6389    6452     +63

>> So we're adding a little overhead to every user's Linux machine for all
>> time.  In return for which the developers get a little convenience and
>> maintainability.
> 
> I think maintainability is of importance. Is there any measurable slowdown?
> Or are we only worried about the text size increase?
> 
>> 
>> Is it worth it?
> 
> This is being done all over the kernel. Perhaps we should look at ways to
> make the generic infrastructure more performant?

Yeah I don't think the guard construct in this case should be doing anything
here that wouldn't allow the compiler to compile to the exactly same result
as before? Either there's some problem with the infra, or we're just victim
of compiler heuristics. In both cases imho worth looking into rather than
rejecting the construct.

> -- Steve

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Agreed that it would be good to investigate the issue further, but expressed concerns about penalizing billions of machines for the sake of maintainability.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 6 Mar 2026 19:24:56 +0100 Vlastimil Babka <vbabka@kernel.org> wrote:

> >> 
> >> Is it worth it?
> > 
> > This is being done all over the kernel. Perhaps we should look at ways to
> > make the generic infrastructure more performant?
> 
> Yeah I don't think the guard construct in this case should be doing anything
> here that wouldn't allow the compiler to compile to the exactly same result
> as before? Either there's some problem with the infra, or we're just victim
> of compiler heuristics.

Sure, it'd be good to figure this out.

> In both cases imho worth looking into rather than
> rejecting the construct.

I'm not enjoying the ides of penalizing billions of machines all of the
time in order to make life a little easier for the developers.  Seems
like a poor tradeoff.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary: Defended the use of zone lock guards, arguing that maintainability is an important consideration and that any potential bugs due to lack of maintainability would also affect billions of machines.

[ Adding Peter ]

On Fri, 6 Mar 2026 09:53:36 -0800
Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org> wrote:

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 5/5] mm: add tracepoints for zone lock

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 4/4] mm: add tracepoints for zone lock
[PATCH v3 5/5] mm: add tracepoints for zone lock
[PATCH v4 5/5] mm: add tracepoints for zone lock

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds tracepoints to the Linux kernel's zone lock acquire and release operations, allowing for tracing of these events when CONFIG_TRACING is enabled. The implementation uses a lightweight inline helper to check if tracing is active, and calls an out-of-line helper when tracing is enabled. When tracing is disabled, the helpers compile to empty inline stubs, leaving the fast path unaffected.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the zone lock tracepoints do not handle the case where the zone is being freed, which can lead to a use-after-free error

The reviewer questioned the use of a boolean 'success' parameter in __zone_lock_trace_acquire_returned(), suggesting that it should always be set to true and questioning its purpose.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 27 Feb 2026 16:00:27 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

---

Why the "success" variable and not just:

	__zone_lock_trace_acquire_returned(zone, true);

 ?

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary: Gave Reviewed-by
review_comments.raw_body:
On 2/27/26 17:00, Dmitry Ilvokhin wrote:
> Add tracepoint instrumentation to zone lock acquire/release operations
> via the previously introduced wrappers.
> 
> The implementation follows the mmap_lock tracepoint pattern: a
> lightweight inline helper checks whether the tracepoint is enabled and
> calls into an out-of-line helper when tracing is active. When
> CONFIG_TRACING is disabled, helpers compile to empty inline stubs.
> 
> The fast path is unaffected when tracing is disabled.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
> Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

Agree with Steven; otherwise

Reviewed-by: Vlastimil Babka (SUSE) <vbabka@kernel.org>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 3/5] mm: convert compaction to zone lock wrappers

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 3/4] mm: convert compaction to zone lock wrappers
[PATCH v3 3/5] mm: convert compaction to zone lock wrappers
[PATCH v4 3/5] mm: convert compaction to zone lock wrappers

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series converts the compaction code to use zone lock wrappers, replacing direct access to spinlocks. This change is necessary because the zone lock operations are now wrapped and cannot be accessed directly by compact_lock_irqsave(). The patch splits the helper into two functions: compact_zone_lock_irqsave() and compact_lruvec_lock_irqsave(), duplicating shared logic for the two call sites.

review_comments.author: SeongJae Park
review_comments.summary: Reviewed the patch and found it to be a good change. The new functions are well-written and follow the existing logic.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 27 Feb 2026 16:00:25 +0000 Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

> Compaction uses compact_lock_irqsave(), which currently operates
> on a raw spinlock_t pointer so it can be used for both zone->lock
> and lruvec->lru_lock. Since zone lock operations are now wrapped,
> compact_lock_irqsave() can no longer directly operate on a
> spinlock_t when the lock belongs to a zone.
> 
> Split the helper into compact_zone_lock_irqsave() and
> compact_lruvec_lock_irqsave(), duplicating the small amount of
> shared logic. As there are only two call sites and both statically
> know the lock type, this avoids introducing additional abstraction
> or runtime dispatch in the compaction path.
> 
> No functional change intended.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
> Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

Reviewed-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>


Thanks,
SJ

[...]

review_comments.author: Vlastimil Babka (SUSE)
review_comments.summary: Also reviewed the patch and found it to be a good change. The new functions are well-written and follow the existing logic.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 4/5] mm: rename zone->lock to zone->_lock

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 4/5] mm: rename zone->lock to zone->_lock
[PATCH v4 4/5] mm: rename zone->lock to zone->_lock

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch renames the 'lock' field in the Linux kernel's memory management (mm) code to '_lock'. The rename is intended to break direct users of the lock at compile time, forcing them to use wrappers and tracing infrastructure. This change does not introduce any functional changes but rather enforces the use of standardized locking mechanisms. The patch affects various files in the mm directory, including include/linux/mmzone.h, include/linux/mmzone_lock.h, and several C files such as compaction.c and page_alloc.c.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by
review_comments.raw_body:
On 2/27/26 17:00, Dmitry Ilvokhin wrote:
> This intentionally breaks direct users of zone->lock at compile time so
> all call sites are converted to the zone lock wrappers. Without the
> rename, present and future out-of-tree code could continue using
> spin_lock(&zone->lock) and bypass the wrappers and tracing
> infrastructure.
> 
> No functional change intended.
> 
> Suggested-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
> Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
> Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
> Acked-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
> ---

Acked-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the rename of zone->lock to zone->_lock may cause issues due to existing lock ordering assumptions in some code paths.
review_comments.raw_body:
Acked-by: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>

Best Regards,
Yan, Zi

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that there are remaining instances of 'zone->lock' in comments and suggested they be updated to use the new zone lock wrappers.
review_comments.raw_body:
I see some more instances of 'zone->lock' in comments in
include/linux/mmzone.h and under Documentation/ but otherwise LGTM.

Reviewed-by: Vlastimil Babka (SUSE) <vbabka@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton noted that his previous fix for the rename of zone->lock to zone->_lock was lost in the patch submission and requested it be reapplied.
review_comments.raw_body:
I fixed (most of) that in the previous version but my fix was lost.


 include/linux/mmzone.h |   10 +++++-----
 1 file changed, 5 insertions(+), 5 deletions(-)

--- a/include/linux/mmzone.h~mm-rename-zone-lock-to-zone-_lock-fix
+++ a/include/linux/mmzone.h
@@ -1037,12 +1037,12 @@ struct zone {
 	 * Locking rules:
 	 *
 	 * zone_start_pfn and spanned_pages are protected by span_seqlock.
-	 * It is a seqlock because it has to be read outside of zone->lock,
+	 * It is a seqlock because it has to be read outside of zone_lock,
 	 * and it is done in the main allocator path.  But, it is written
 	 * quite infrequently.
 	 *
-	 * The span_seq lock is declared along with zone->lock because it is
-	 * frequently read in proximity to zone->lock.  It's good to
+	 * The span_seq lock is declared along with zone_lock because it is
+	 * frequently read in proximity to zone_lock.  It's good to
 	 * give them a chance of being in the same cacheline.
 	 *
 	 * Write access to present_pages at runtime should be protected by
@@ -1065,7 +1065,7 @@ struct zone {
 	/*
 	 * Number of isolated pageblock. It is used to solve incorrect
 	 * freepage counting problem due to racy retrieving migratetype
-	 * of pageblock. Protected by zone->lock.
+	 * of pageblock. Protected by zone_lock.
 	 */
 	unsigned long		nr_isolate_pageblock;
 #endif
@@ -1502,7 +1502,7 @@ typedef struct pglist_data {
 	 * manipulate node_size_lock without checking for CONFIG_MEMORY_HOTPLUG
 	 * or CONFIG_DEFERRED_STRUCT_PAGE_INIT.
 	 *
-	 * Nests above zone->lock and zone->span_seqlock
+	 * Nests above zone_lock and zone->span_seqlock
 	 */
 	spinlock_t node_size_lock;
 #endif
_

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that some 'zone->lock' references remain in Documentation and offered to make adjustments, indicating a willingness to address any remaining issues.

Author acknowledged that there are remaining references to zone->lock in the code and submitted a new patch (v5) to fix these issues.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the fixups, Andrew.

I still see a few 'zone->lock' references in Documentation remain on
mm-new. This patch cleans them up, as noted by Vlastimil.

I'm happy to adjust this patch if anything else needs attention.

---

From: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
Date: Tue, 3 Mar 2026 06:13:13 -0800
Subject: [PATCH] mm: fix remaining zone->lock references

Signed-off-by: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>
---
 Documentation/mm/physical_memory.rst | 4 ++--
 Documentation/trace/events-kmem.rst  | 8 ++++----
 2 files changed, 6 insertions(+), 6 deletions(-)

diff --git a/Documentation/mm/physical_memory.rst b/Documentation/mm/physical_memory.rst
index b76183545e5b..e344f93515b6 100644
--- a/Documentation/mm/physical_memory.rst
+++ b/Documentation/mm/physical_memory.rst
@@ -500,11 +500,11 @@ General
 ``nr_isolate_pageblock``
   Number of isolated pageblocks. It is used to solve incorrect freepage counting
   problem due to racy retrieving migratetype of pageblock. Protected by
-  ``zone->lock``. Defined only when ``CONFIG_MEMORY_ISOLATION`` is enabled.
+  ``zone_lock``. Defined only when ``CONFIG_MEMORY_ISOLATION`` is enabled.
 
 ``span_seqlock``
   The seqlock to protect ``zone_start_pfn`` and ``spanned_pages``. It is a
-  seqlock because it has to be read outside of ``zone->lock``, and it is done in
+  seqlock because it has to be read outside of ``zone_lock``, and it is done in
   the main allocator path. However, the seqlock is written quite infrequently.
   Defined only when ``CONFIG_MEMORY_HOTPLUG`` is enabled.
 
diff --git a/Documentation/trace/events-kmem.rst b/Documentation/trace/events-kmem.rst
index 68fa75247488..3c20a972de27 100644
--- a/Documentation/trace/events-kmem.rst
+++ b/Documentation/trace/events-kmem.rst
@@ -57,7 +57,7 @@ the per-CPU allocator (high performance) or the buddy allocator.
 
 If pages are allocated directly from the buddy allocator, the
 mm_page_alloc_zone_locked event is triggered. This event is important as high
-amounts of activity imply high activity on the zone->lock. Taking this lock
+amounts of activity imply high activity on the zone_lock. Taking this lock
 impairs performance by disabling interrupts, dirtying cache lines between
 CPUs and serialising many CPUs.
 
@@ -79,11 +79,11 @@ contention on the lruvec->lru_lock.
   mm_page_pcpu_drain		page=%p pfn=%lu order=%d cpu=%d migratetype=%d
 
 In front of the page allocator is a per-cpu page allocator. It exists only
-for order-0 pages, reduces contention on the zone->lock and reduces the
+for order-0 pages, reduces contention on the zone_lock and reduces the
 amount of writing on struct page.
 
 When a per-CPU list is empty or pages of the wrong type are allocated,
-the zone->lock will be taken once and the per-CPU list refilled. The event
+the zone_lock will be taken once and the per-CPU list refilled. The event
 triggered is mm_page_alloc_zone_locked for each page allocated with the
 event indicating whether it is for a percpu_refill or not.
 
@@ -92,7 +92,7 @@ which triggers a mm_page_pcpu_drain event.
 
 The individual nature of the events is so that pages can be tracked
 between allocation and freeing. A number of drain or refill pages that occur
-consecutively imply the zone->lock being taken once. Large amounts of per-CPU
+consecutively imply the zone_lock being taken once. Large amounts of per-CPU
 refills and drains could imply an imbalance between CPUs where too much work
 is being concentrated in one place. It could also indicate that the per-CPU
 lists should be a larger size. Finally, large amounts of refills on one CPU
-- 
2.47.3

review_comments.author: SeongJae Park
review_comments.summary: Reviewer SeongJae Park questioned whether the change from 'zone->lock' to 'zone_lock' was intentional, noting that Andrew Morton made a similar change elsewhere
review_comments.raw_body:
Dmitry's original patch [1] was doing 's/zone->lock/zone->_lock/', which aligns
to my expectation.  But this patch is doing 's/zone->lock/zone_lock/'.  Same
for the rest of this patch.

I was initially thinking this is just a mistake, but I also found Andrew is
doing same change [2], so I'm bit confused.  Is this an intentional change?

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/d61500c5784c64e971f4d328c57639303c475f81.1772206930.git.d@ilvokhin.com
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/20260302143743.220eed4feb36d7572fe726cc@linux-foundation.org


Thanks,
SJ

[...]

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that there is an inconsistency between the mechanical rename of zone->lock to zone->_lock and documentation references to zone_lock, agreed to adjust either way for consistency.
review_comments.raw_body:
Good catch, thanks for pointing this out, SJ.

Originally the mechanical rename was indeed zone->lock -> zone->_lock.
However, in Documentation I intentionally switched references to
zone_lock instead of zone->_lock. The reasoning is that _lock is now an
internal implementation detail, and direct access is discouraged. The
intended interface is via the zone_lock_*() / zone_unlock_*() wrappers,
so referencing zone_lock in documentation felt more appropriate than
mentioning the private struct field (zone->_lock).

That said, I agree this creates inconsistency with the mechanical
rename, and I'm happy to adjust either way: either consistently refer
to the wrapper API, or keep documentation aligned with zone->_lock.

I slightly prefer referring to the wrapper API, but don't have a strong
preference as long as we're consistent.

review_comments.author: SeongJae Park
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer SeongJae Park suggested rewording documentation to explicitly mention the zone locking helpers, citing a potential lack of awareness about the intention behind 'zone_lock'.

Reviewer suggested providing additional context in the zone lock wrappers for better readability
review_comments.raw_body:
Thank you for this nice and kind clarification, Dmitry!  I agree mentioning
zone_[un]lock_*() helpers instead of the hidden member (zone->_lock) can be
better.

But, I'm concerned if people like me might not aware the intention under
'zone_lock'.  If there is a well-known convention that allows people to know it
is for 'zone_[un]lock_*()' helpers, making it more clear would be nice, in my
humble opinion.  If there is such a convention but I'm just missing it, please
ignore.  If I'm not, for eaxmaple,

"protected by ``zone->lock``" could be re-wrote to
"protected by ``zone_[un]lock_*()`` locking helpers" or,
"protected by zone lock helper functions (``zone_[un]lock_*()``)" ?

---

I also think both approaches are good.  But for the wrapper approach, I think
giving more contexts rather than just ``zone_lock`` to readers would be nice.


Thanks,
SJ

[...]

review_comments.author: SeongJae Park
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer suggested rewording the patch description to make it more concise and accessible, specifically mentioning that the zone lock is protected by functions in mmzone_lock.h

Reviewer noted that the zone lock renaming may cause issues due to existing code using spin_lock(&zone->lock), and requested a check for this scenario.
review_comments.raw_body:
Maybe too verbose and people who not used to regex might be confused.
Mentioning mmzone_lock.h might be better?  E.g.,

    protected by functions in mmzone_lock.h

---

Thanks,
SJ

[...]

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested renaming zone->_lock back to zone->lock for consistency in comments and documentation, citing ease of readability
review_comments.raw_body:
Grep tells me that we also have comments mentioning simply "zone lock", btw.
And it's also a term used often in informal conversations. Maybe we could
just standardize on that in comments/documentations as it's easier to read.
Discovering that the field is called _lock and that wrappers should be used,
is hopefully not that difficult.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch replaces direct zone lock acquire and release operations in various kernel modules with newly introduced wrappers. The changes are mechanical substitutions only, without any functional changes or modifications to locking semantics or ordering. This is the second of five patches aimed at converting zone lock users to wrappers.

did.


Besides that

Acked-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>

-- 
Cheers,

David

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch introduces thin wrappers around zone lock acquire and release operations, allowing for future instrumentation or debugging hooks to be added without modifying individual call sites. The wrappers are introduced in preparation for subsequent patches and do not yet have any functional changes.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 0/5] mm: zone lock tracepoint instrumentation

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/4] mm: zone lock tracepoint instrumentation
[PATCH v3 0/5] mm: zone lock tracepoint instrumentation
[PATCH v4 0/5] mm: zone lock tracepoint instrumentation

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds tracepoint instrumentation to zone lock acquire and release operations in the Linux kernel. The implementation uses a lightweight inline helper to check if tracing is enabled, and calls an out-of-line helper when tracing is active. When CONFIG_TRACING is disabled, the helpers compile to empty inline stubs, leaving the fast path unaffected. This patch introduces three new tracepoints: zone_lock_start_locking, zone_lock_acquire_returned, and zone_lock_released.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the zone lock tracepoints do not handle the case where CONFIG_TRACING is disabled, and suggested adding a check for this condition to avoid unnecessary function calls

The reviewer questioned the use of a boolean 'success' parameter in __zone_lock_trace_acquire_returned(), suggesting that it should always be set to true and proposing a simplified version.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 27 Feb 2026 16:00:27 +0000
Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com> wrote:

---

Why the "success" variable and not just:

	__zone_lock_trace_acquire_returned(zone, true);

 ?

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [patch 04/14] x86/irq: Make irqstats array based
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch introduces changes to the irqstats array, but some reviewers have raised concerns about potential issues and typos.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about potential build breakage due to a typo in HYPERV_REENLIGHTMENT and a similar issue with the text description. Also pointed out that the patch drops prec-based alignment for ERR and MIS.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v4 0/5] mm: zone lock tracepoint instrumentation
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch series provides lightweight observability for zone locks in production environments, addressing the limitations of CONFIG_LOCK_STAT as a debug facility. It aims to improve the ability to monitor and diagnose lock contention issues without impacting performance. The approach taken is specific to zone locks, but an alternative direction is explored through a separate RFC that proposes adding a contended_release tracepoint for sleepable locks to enable more generic lock tracing infrastructure.

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary:
reviewer expressed frustration that the patch series targets specific locks (mmap and zone) instead of addressing generic lock instrumentation, suggesting that this approach is too heavyweight and inefficient

reviewer pointed out that LOCK_STAT doesn't affect the unlock path, but instead only modifies the acquire side by calling lock_acquired() and lock_contended(), suggesting the use of tracepoint_enabled() and friends

Reviewer noted that they lack expertise in the locking code and cannot provide a thorough review
review_comments.raw_body:
I read that.  But first it was the mmap lock.  Now it's the zone lock.
Which lock will be next?  This is too heavyweight a procedure to
follow for each lock of interest.

---

This is why we have tracepoint_enabled() and friends.  But ... LOCK_STAT
doesn't affect the unlock path at all.  It only changes the acquire side
to call lock_acquired() (and lock_contended() if the trylock failed).

---

It seems fine to me, but I don't have the depth in the locking code to
give it the thorough review it deserves.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
reviewer suggested improving generic lock tracing infrastructure by adding a contended_release tracepoint to correlate lock holders and waiters, and requested feedback on this direction

reviewer noted that adding generic lightweight lock instrumentation would cause noticeable overhead due to inlined fast paths and increased text size, and suggested exploring a lighter version of LOCK_STAT without LOCKDEP
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, 9 Mar 2026 14:47:29 +0000
Matthew Wilcox <willy@infradead.org> wrote:

---

The biggest issue with making a generic light weight LOCK_STAT is that
locks are extremely optimized. Any addition of generic lock encoding will
cause a noticeable overhead when compiled in, even when disabled.

Most of the lock code is inlined for the fast path. Now if we want to add
lock stats, we would need to add hooks into those inlined paths. This will
undoubtedly increase the size of text, which will have an impact on I$.

The other issue is the data we store for the lock. A lock is usually just a
word (or long) in size, embedded in a structure. LOCKDEP and LOCK_STAT adds
a key per lock. This increases the data size of the kernel.

LOCK_STAT was designed on top of LOCKDEP. Perhaps a lighter version of
LOCK_STAT could be designed without LOCKDEP, but it would be a project on
its own.

-- Steve

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary:
Matthew Wilcox pointed out that the current Debian kernel configuration leads to a 'call' instruction to acquire a spinlock, which contradicts the claim that CONFIG_LOCK_STAT is too heavyweight for production environments.

reviewer suggested using the return address to determine which lock is being accessed, instead of embedding a key in each lock
review_comments.raw_body:
I'm not sure that's true.  Taking the current Debian kernel config
leads to a "call" instruction to acquire a spinlock:

void __insert_inode_hash(struct inode *inode, unsigned long hashval)
{
        struct hlist_head *b = inode_hashtable + hash(inode->i_sb, hashval);

        spin_lock(&inode_hash_lock);
        spin_lock(&inode->i_lock);
        hlist_add_head_rcu(&inode->i_hash, b);
        spin_unlock(&inode->i_lock);
        spin_unlock(&inode_hash_lock);
}

compiles to:

[...]
     280:       23 35 00 00 00 00       and    0x0(%rip),%esi        # 286 <__insert_inode_hash+0x56>
                        282: R_X86_64_PC32      .data..ro_after_init+0x10
     286:       48 8d 2c f0             lea    (%rax,%rsi,8),%rbp
     28a:       e8 00 00 00 00          call   28f <__insert_inode_hash+0x5f>
                        28b: R_X86_64_PLT32     _raw_spin_lock-0x4
     28f:       4c 89 e7                mov    %r12,%rdi
     292:       e8 00 00 00 00          call   297 <__insert_inode_hash+0x67>
                        293: R_X86_64_PLT32     _raw_spin_lock-0x4
[...]

Debian doesn't do anything too weird here:

#
# Lock Debugging (spinlocks, mutexes, etc...)
#
CONFIG_LOCK_DEBUGGING_SUPPORT=y
# CONFIG_PROVE_LOCKING is not set
# CONFIG_LOCK_STAT is not set
# CONFIG_DEBUG_RT_MUTEXES is not set
# CONFIG_DEBUG_SPINLOCK is not set
# CONFIG_DEBUG_MUTEXES is not set
# CONFIG_DEBUG_WW_MUTEX_SLOWPATH is not set
# CONFIG_DEBUG_RWSEMS is not set
# CONFIG_DEBUG_LOCK_ALLOC is not set
# CONFIG_DEBUG_ATOMIC_SLEEP is not set
# CONFIG_DEBUG_LOCKING_API_SELFTESTS is not set
# CONFIG_LOCK_TORTURE_TEST is not set
# CONFIG_WW_MUTEX_SELFTEST is not set
# CONFIG_SCF_TORTURE_TEST is not set
# CONFIG_CSD_LOCK_WAIT_DEBUG is not set

(The spinlock code is too complex for me to follow what config options
influence whether it's a function call; you probably have enough of it
in your head that you'd know)

---

It does, but perhaps for a light weight lockstat, we could do better
than that.  For example it could use the return address to look up
which lock is being accessed rather than embedding a key in each lock.

review_comments.author: Steven Rostedt
review_comments.summary:
reviewer suggested improving generic lock tracing infrastructure by adding a contended_release tracepoint to correlate lock holders and waiters, and requested feedback on whether this direction makes sense

reviewer noted that only a few architectures inline spinlocks anymore and suggested that adding code to spinlocks may not have a significant impact on instruction cache

reviewer expressed frustration with having to constantly relearn the code due to lack of documentation or understanding

reviewer expressed agreement with the patch and suggested an alternative approach to improve generic lock instrumentation
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, 9 Mar 2026 20:45:31 +0000
Matthew Wilcox <willy@infradead.org> wrote:

---

Ah, you're correct. Looks like it's an arch specific thing. I was going
back to my memory from around 2006, but it appears that only a few archs
inline spinlocks anymore. Thomas made it a bit easier to see what does and
does not do that (in 2009).

  6beb000923882 ("locking: Make inlining decision Kconfig based")

So, perhaps adding code to the spinlocks will not be as much of a hit on I$.

---

Yeah, I feel like I'm always relearning the code every time I have to jump
in and understand it again.

---

Right.

-- Steve
